intro: Porting applications from C# to Python shifts development from a strictly structured, type-safe enterprise framework (.NET) to a highly dynamic, interpreted language focused on expressiveness and rapid iteration. Developers trade rigid class contracts, JIT compilation, and predictable performance for dynamic duck-typing, lightweight scripting syntax, and an expansive data science ecosystem. This change eliminates verbose compilation workflows at the expense of runtime type safety and raw execution speed.

diffs.from: Static, nominal, strong typing system featuring compile-time checks, generics, and explicit nullability context controls.
diffs.to: Dynamic, strong typing system utilizing duck-typing rules, runtime evaluation, and optional gradual type hints (mypy).

diffs.from: Brace-scoped, verbose, object-oriented syntax requiring strict class encapsulation, access modifiers, and structural semicolons.
diffs.to: Indentation-scoped, clean, minimal layout supporting multi-paradigm execution (procedural, functional, object-oriented) without boilerplate.

diffs.from: Managed via the .NET Generational Garbage Collector, optimizing heap object structures and stack value types (structs).
diffs.to: Managed via Reference Counting augmented by a cyclical generational garbage collector, wrapping all primitives as heap objects.

diffs.from: Advanced async/await keyword integration backed by the thread-pool-driven Task Parallel Library (TPL) engine.
diffs.to: Single-threaded event loop architecture managed via asyncio, constrained historically by the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL).

diffs.from: Deep enterprise-focused System namespaces handling high-performance networks, reflection, cryptography, and parallel queries.
diffs.to: Extensive 'batteries included' library providing immediate solutions for scripting, text parsing, math, and rapid network prototyping.

examples.title: LINQ Filter vs List Comprehension
examples.description: Demonstrates converting a C# LINQ query statement into a native, high-performance Python list comprehension block.
examples.toCode:
def get_evens(numbers: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    return [n for n in numbers if n % 2 == 0]

examples.title: Asynchronous Task Processing
examples.description: Contrasts C# Task operations with Python asyncio execution to fetch external text resources asynchronously.
examples.toCode:
import asyncio
import aiohttp

async def fetch_data_async(url: str) -> str:
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.get(url) as response:
            return await response.text()

tips.title: Enforce Gradual Type Hinting
tips.body: Maintain architectural reliability when leaving C# by utilizing Python's type hints and running static checkers like `mypy` or `pyright` inside your continuous integration pipelines.

tips.title: Swap LINQ for Comprehensions
tips.body: Avoid searching for direct LINQ method chains in Python. Master list, dictionary, and generator comprehensions to write idiomatic and highly performant collection mutations.

tips.title: Beware the Single Event Loop
tips.body: Unlike C# TPL which dispatches async tasks across multiple CPU cores via thread pools, Python's `asyncio` runs on a single thread. CPU-bound work must be explicitly delegated to `ProcessPoolExecutor` blocks.

tips.title: Leverage Dictionaries Over Classes
tips.body: C# relies heavily on structures and anonymous classes for temporary data structures. In Python, use lightweight dictionaries, `namedtuple`, or `dataclasses` to avoid the overhead of heavy class boilerplate.
